1:
The chapter opens by introducing the man on whom the whole story will turn, and the witnesses linger over each phrase of his introduction. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the notice is a narrative necessity: the account "commences with a statement which was necessary in order to make it perfectly intelligible," namely that Boaz, to whose field Ruth will shortly come, was a relative of Naomi through her deceased husband Elimelech. He is "a mighty man of wealth," and Adam Clarke adds that some have supposed him one of the judges of Israel — at the least "a man of considerable property." Matthew Henry delights in the Chaldee paraphrase, which reads "mighty in the law": if Boaz was both, "it was a most rare and excellent conjunction, to be mighty in wealth and mighty in the scriptures too; those that are so are mighty indeed." His very name preaches — Boaz, "in him is strength." Charles Ellicott and Henry trace the pedigree from Ruth 4:18: grandson of Nahshon, prince of Judah in the wilderness, and son of Salmon, "probably a younger son, by Rahab, the harlot of Jericho" — Ellicott meeting the chronological difficulty by supposing gaps in the genealogy's earlier portion, or children born "of their fathers' old age."

The more searching question the witnesses raise is why this wealthy kinsman and the destitute widow have had nothing to do with each other. John Gill notes both silences: Naomi made no application to him for assistance "though well known to her," which he attributes to her modesty, "being loath to be troublesome to him, especially as he was a relation, not of her own family, but of her husband's"; and, "what is more strange," the generous Boaz "had taken no notice of her" — perhaps, Gill charitably suggests, because he was not acquainted with their indigent circumstances. Henry turns the double silence into a double lesson. On one side, "Boaz, though a rich and great man, had poor relations. Every branch of the tree is not a top-branch," and the great should not be ashamed to own kindred that are "mean and despised, lest they be found therein proud, scornful, and unnatural." On the other, Naomi, "though a poor contemptible widow, had rich relations, whom yet she boasted not of, nor was burdensome to, nor expected any thing from."

Out of this the witnesses draw a common theology of providence. Henry states it most fully: it is "the wise providence of God that makes the difference" between rich and poor kindred, a providence in which we ought to acquiesce; "to be proud of our relation to such is a great sin, and to trust to it is great folly." Keil and Delitzsch, surveying the chapter whole, already see where the quiet notice is heading: Ruth will come by chance to the field of this very kinsman, be spoken to kindly, and return to Naomi with a plentiful gleaning, learning only afterward that her benefactor is a relation. The verse thus plants, unnoticed by every actor in the story, the seed of redemption. The kinsman is in place — wealthy, godly, and near — before Ruth has gleaned a single ear; and the line that runs through Rahab to Boaz will run on through Ruth to David and to Christ (Matt. 1:5).
2:
Ruth's simple request — "Let me now go to the field, and glean ears of corn" — draws from the witnesses two lines of comment: the law that made gleaning possible, and the character that made it beautiful. On the legal side, Keil and Delitzsch set out the Mosaic provision: Lev. 19:9, Lev. 23:22, and Deut. 24:19 "expressly secured to the poor the right to glean in the harvest fields, and prohibited the owners from gleaning themselves; but hard-hearted farmers and reapers threw obstacles in the way of the poor, and even forbade their gleaning altogether." That is why Ruth proposes to glean "after him in whose sight I shall find grace" — she will follow whoever generously allows it. Adam Clarke agrees that "notwithstanding the law of Moses, the gleaners might be prevented by the owner of the field," and observes that she "did not mean Boaz," whom she had never met, but any friendly reaper; he adds a curiosity of comparative law, tracing the English custom of gleaning down to a court judgment that no such right exists at common law. John Wesley keeps the balance exact: gleaning was permitted to the poor and the stranger, yet "though it was their duty to permit this, she thought it might perhaps be denied her; at least, that it became her modestly and humbly to acknowledge their kindness herein."

On the side of character the voices pile up their praises. John Gill commends a woman "unwilling to live an idle life, and ready to do any thing for the support of her life, and of her ancient mother-in-law" — industrious and virtuous. Charles Ellicott finds the whole portrait here: "Energy, honesty of purpose, and loyalty are alike evinced"; she neither shrinks from humbling work nor hankers "after her old home in the land of Moab and the plenty there." Matthew Henry expands this into a fourfold example. Of humility: "High spirits can more easily starve than stoop; Ruth was none of those" — those well born know not what mean employments they may be reduced to (Lam. 4:5). Of industry: she was none of those who eat the bread of idleness, for no honest labour is a reproach. Of regard to her mother: she "will not go out without letting her know and asking her leave." Of dependence upon Providence: she knows not whose field she will find, "but will trust Providence to raise her up some friend or other that will be kind to her."

The witnesses land together on the manner of the asking. "Poor people must not demand kindness as a debt," Henry writes, "but humbly ask it, and take it as a favour, though in ever so small a matter" — precisely Wesley's point about her modesty before a right the law already gave her. Naomi's brief "Go, my daughter" receives its own weight: the aged widow consents to be served, and the young widow insists on serving. In Ruth's first recorded initiative the witnesses see faith working through diligence — the proselyte who took refuge under the wings of Israel's God now goes out, empty-handed and unashamed, to discover through whom that God will feed her.
3:
"Her hap was to light on a part of the field belonging unto Boaz" — and on this word "hap" the witnesses are unanimous and eloquent. Keil and Delitzsch supply the Hebrew: literally "her chance chanced to hit upon the field," and they underline that she went "without her knowing the owner of the field, or being at all aware of his connection with Elimelech." Adam Clarke compresses the doctrine into a phrase: "she was accidentally or providentially led" — both words true at their own level. John Wesley supplies the distinction the others assume: "It was a chance in reference to second causes, but ordered by God's providence. God wisely orders small events, even those that seem altogether contingent. Many a great affair is brought about by a little turn, fortuitous as to men, but designed by God." Charles Ellicott gives the same truth its most graceful form: "A chance in outward seeming, yet a clear shaping of her course by unseen hands. Her steps were divinely guided to a certain field, that God's good purposes should be worked out."

Matthew Henry dwells on how it looked from inside Ruth's experience: "To her it seemed casual. She knew not whose field it was, nor had she any reason for going to that more than any other, and therefore it is said to be her hap; but Providence directed her steps to this field." He draws the general rule that what seems altogether contingent serves God's own glory and the good of his people. John Gill adds a rabbinic curiosity from the Midrash — that Ruth marked the ways as she went, so that she might not mistake the road home — a detail that, whatever its worth, catches the truth that she was a stranger walking unfamiliar paths with no guide but the God she had chosen in Ruth 1:16.

What the witnesses together refuse is any flattening of the verse to either pole. They will not deny the reality of the contingency — Ruth genuinely chose no field and knew no owner; nor will they leave it contingent — for on this "little turn" hangs the marriage of chapters three and four, the birth of Obed, the house of David, and the genealogy of Matt. 1:5. The narrator's studied phrase is thus, in their reading, a quiet irony: he calls it chance precisely so that the reader will see it was nothing of the kind. The pastoral yield is the one Henry and Wesley both press: since God governs the smallest and most seemingly fortuitous events, the believer may go out, as Ruth did, into an ordinary day's necessity — asking leave, expecting little — and find that mercy has gone ahead and chosen the field. Those who trust Providence while they are diligent in duty discover, sooner or later, that their hap was their Father's plan.
4:
The verse records only a greeting, yet the witnesses find in it a whole portrait of a godly household. Adam Clarke lingers over the very words exchanged: Boaz says "Jehovah be with you!" and the reapers answer, "May Jehovah bless thee!" — and he cannot restrain the sigh, "Can a pious mind read these godly salutations without wishing for a return of those simple primitive times?" He paraphrases the blessing in both directions: God preserving the workers and strengthening them for their task; God blessing the master with the increase of the field and grace to use his bounty to the Giver's glory. Matthew Henry reads the exchange as mutual respect steeped in shared piety: Boaz "did not fall a chiding them, as if he came only to find fault and exercise his authority, but he prayed for them"; and they, far from cursing him behind his back "as some ill-natured servants that hate their master's eye," returned his courtesy. "Things are likely to go on well in a house where there is such good-will as this between master and servants." He notes that it was the usual custom to wish reapers good speed (Ps. 129:7–8), and adds the caution that such forms must not "degenerate into formality, lest in them we take the name of the Lord our God in vain."

The master's presence in his own field draws its own commentary. John Gill observes that though Boaz was a man of great wealth, "he did not think it below him to go into his field, and look after his servants," which showed his diligence and industry as well as his humility; and he garnishes the point from the classics — Homer's king standing cheerful among his reapers, sceptre in hand; Pliny's ancient saying that "the eye of the master is the most fruitful thing in the field"; the Persian who answered that nothing fattens a horse like the master's eye. Charles Ellicott draws the character sketch: Boaz comes to supervise, "but it is not in a fault-finding spirit, but with true courtesy and friendliness; nor is it a frivolous jesting manner that he displays, but with gravity and soberness he presents a true gentleman in his intercourse with his inferiors."

John Wesley presses the point that stings: master and men "expressed their piety, even in their civil conversation, and worldly transactions; which now so many are ashamed of." That is the witnesses' common ground. Religion in Bethlehem was not confined to sanctuary or sabbath; it seasoned the harvest field, and the greeting of employer and employed was mutual prayer. Henry generalizes that true religion "will form kind masters and faithful servants, and cause harmony in families," and that a stranger like Ruth, listening at the field's edge, would form her estimate of Israel's God from precisely such converse. The scene has often been set against the coarse speech of later harvest fields; the witnesses insist the difference is not manners but grace. Where God is acknowledged between master and servant, kindness to the stranger — which the rest of the chapter unfolds — comes naturally.
5:
"Whose damsel is this?" The question is small, but the witnesses use it to open up the whole economy of an ancient harvest. John Gill describes the man to whom it is put: the servant "set over the reapers" directed each labourer's task, saw that the work was well done, cared for the provisions, and paid the wages when the work was over; Josephus calls him the overseer of the field, and Jewish tradition — with its love of precision — sets him over two and forty persons. Adam Clarke identifies him as "a kind of steward or hind who had the under management of the estate," and illustrates the office at length from the harvest scene on the shield of Achilles: reapers with their sickles, binders with the sheaves, boys gathering the handfuls behind them, and amid them the master standing "staff in hand... enjoying, mute, the order of the field" — a picture in which, Clarke thinks, Homer has unwittingly drawn Boaz's harvest as faithfully as any in Greece.

Matthew Henry, characteristically, moralizes the office itself. "In great families it is requisite there should be one to oversee the rest of the servants, and appoint to each their portion both of work and meat" — and then the sudden elevation: "Ministers are such servants in God's house, and it is requisite that they be both wise and faithful, and show their Lord all things, as he here." The steward who can answer his master's question about one stranger in the field becomes a figure of the pastor who knows the flock and can give account of it.

The question itself receives equal weight. Boaz has been in his field only moments, has greeted his reapers, and already his eye has found the one figure who does not belong. Keil and Delitzsch suggest that his interest was not idle: the report he was about to receive of Ruth's modesty and diligence "could only strengthen Boaz in his purpose, which he had probably already formed from his affection as a relation towards Naomi, to make the acquaintance of her daughter-in-law, and speak kindly to her." On this reading the question is the first visible motion of a kindness already forming. The witnesses also note what the inquiry implies about Boaz's ordered household: he knows his own people well enough that a newcomer stands out, and he asks before he acts — neither ignoring the stranger nor presuming upon her. In a chapter that will turn entirely on his treatment of one poor gleaner, the first thing Boaz does is seek her name from the man appointed to know it. Authority, in the witnesses' reading, is exercised here as it ought always to be: through knowledge, order, and personal notice of the least person on the ground.
6:
The overseer's answer is a single sentence — "It is the Moabitish damsel that came back with Naomi out of the country of Moab" — and John Gill notes the qualification that makes it trustworthy: the servant "had taken a great deal of notice of Ruth, and had conversed with her, and so was capable of giving answers to his master's question." He does not guess; he reports what he has watched and heard. The answer identifies Ruth by the two facts that defined her in Bethlehem's eyes: her nation and her loyalty. She is Moabitish — a foreigner, daughter of a people outside the covenant; and she "came back with Naomi" — the town had watched her arrive (Ruth 1:22), and her story had evidently travelled with her. In one breath the servant names both her disadvantage and her glory.

Matthew Henry draws out how much this "fair account" accomplished, for the steward's report was "proper to recommend her to his favour." It told Boaz that she was a stranger, and therefore among those whom the law entitled to gather the gleanings of the harvest (Lev. 19:9–10); that she was allied to his own family, having come back with Naomi, the wife of his kinsman Elimelech; and that she was a proselyte, "for she came out of the country of Moab to settle in the land of Israel." Every clause of the answer, in other words, gave Boaz a reason to be kind. Henry turns the observation into counsel for every household: "Servants should be just in the character and reports they give to their masters, and take heed they do not misrepresent any person, nor without cause discourage their master's charity." A censorious steward could have described the same woman as a Moabite intruder presuming on the field; this one told the truth in the order that mercy would wish to hear it.

Keil and Delitzsch observe the effect: the good report of Ruth's modesty and diligence "could only strengthen Boaz in his purpose... to make the acquaintance of her daughter-in-law, and speak kindly to her." The servant's answer is thus the human hinge on which the meeting turns, and Boaz's own words in Ruth 2:11 — "it hath fully been shewed me, all that thou hast done" — trace directly back to testimony of this kind. The witnesses find here a quiet doctrine of reputation. Ruth had sought no notice, yet her faithfulness had preached for her before she spoke a word to Boaz; and God used the honest tongue of an unnamed farm-servant to open the door of her redemption. Character, they conclude, is the one letter of commendation the poor can always carry, and heaven sees that it is delivered.
7:
The overseer continues his report, and the witnesses prize each detail of it. John Gill notes simply that "these are the words of the servant continued, who goes on with the account of Ruth, and her conduct, since she had been in the field" — the master hears the stranger's whole morning from a man who watched it. Three things stand out in the telling. First, her modesty: she asked before she gleaned. John Wesley puts it, "She did not boldly intrude herself, but modestly ask leave of us" — though the law of Deut. 24:19 was on her side, she claimed nothing. Second, her diligence: "she is not retired through idleness," Wesley continues, "for she hath been diligent and constant in her labours." Keil and Delitzsch press the Hebrew particle: she hath continued "from the morning until now," the word rendered "from" being the stronger form — from the very break of day onwards, without intermission. Third, her brief rest: "she tarried a little in the house."

On that last clause the witnesses supply the picture. Wesley explains "the house" as "the little house or tent, which was set up in the fields at these times, and was necessary in those hot countries, where the labourers might retire for a little repose or repast. Being weary with her continued labours, she comes hither to take a little rest." Matthew Henry adds the tender conjecture of some, that in the heat of the day she withdrew for her devotion as well as her repose, and underlines the proportion: "she soon came back to her work, and, except that little intermission, kept close to it all day, though it was not what she had been used to." A woman lately widowed, foreign, and gently reared works a stranger's field from dawn, and her one recorded indulgence is "a little" shade.

The witnesses draw the same conclusion from opposite ends. From below: this is what genuine humility looks like in practice — not self-abasing words, but asking leave, keeping at the work, resting briefly, returning. From above: this is the account God delights to have given of his people by those who watch them. The servant had no idea he was writing Ruth's testimonial for her kinsman-redeemer, still less that his plain sentences would be read wherever the Scriptures go; he only answered his master honestly. Henry's counsel that the industrious poor "are fit to be encouraged" finds here its perfect case. Before Boaz says one word to Ruth, her conduct, faithfully reported, has already spoken for her; and the grace that meets her in the following verses will crown a character that asked nothing but leave to gather what others left behind.
8:
Boaz's first words to Ruth set the tone for everything that follows, and the witnesses hear their gentleness in the very grammar. Keil and Delitzsch render the opening, "Dost thou hear, my daughter?" — that is, "thou hearest, dost thou not?" — and cite the old rhetoricians' rule that such a question affirms blandly: Boaz speaks "with fatherly kindness." The address "my daughter" tells its own tale; Charles Ellicott infers from it "that Boaz was no longer a young man," and that the account he had heard from his servant and from general report, together with "her appearance and behaviour and doubtless a feeling of pity at her condition, had prepossessed him in her favour."

The substance of the speech is a double instruction: do not glean elsewhere, and stay close by my maidens. On the first, the witnesses note that Boaz anticipates and forbids the wandering life of the ordinary gleaner — she is to make his field her home for the whole harvest. On the second, they dwell on his delicacy. Ellicott observes that the Hebrew is literally "cleave to" my maidens — the very word of Gen. 2:24 — and that "the true courtesy of Boaz's character shows itself in the mention of the maidens. He will not have the stranger even run the chance of rudeness, by being away from the company of her own sex." John Wesley is characteristically blunt about the alternative: she is placed with the maidens and "not by the young men, to avoid both occasion of sin, and matter of scandal. Herein he shews his piety and prudence." Adam Clarke fills in the field-craft: the maidens "were probably employed in making bands, and laying on them enough to form a sheaf"; when they had gathered up the scattered handfuls thrown down by the reapers, "Ruth picked up any straggling heads or ears which they had left" — so that keeping near them was also the best station for her gleaning.

What unites the witnesses is admiration for a kindness at once large and detailed. Boaz gives Ruth more than permission: he gives her security for a season, a company of her own sex, a settled place. Wesley and Ellicott together read here a model of practical holiness — piety that thinks about scandal before it arises, courtesy that protects the vulnerable without humiliating them. And several of the witnesses cannot help noticing how the scene begins to exceed its occasion: a wealthy kinsman bidding a poor stranger "abide here fast," not going to another field, cleaving to his household — language that will bear a richer sense when the gleaner becomes a wife, and that Matthew Henry will later turn upon the soul's adherence to its Redeemer, in whose field alone are grace and plenty to be found.
9:
Boaz's care now becomes concrete, and the witnesses trace its two arms: protection and provision. On protection, the charge to the young men is stark — "have I not charged the young men that they shall not touch thee?" Adam Clarke notes that this was "peculiarly necessary, as she was a stranger and unprotected"; a foreign widow alone among harvest labourers was exposed in ways every reader of Judges 19 understands. John Wesley softens nothing in his gloss: "touch" means "so as to offer any incivility or injury to thee" — Boaz forbids not merely violence but rudeness. The witnesses observe that the charge had been given before Ruth knew of it; his protection preceded her awareness of the danger, the mark of a guardianship that is provident rather than reactive.

On provision, the invitation is equally deliberate: "when thou art athirst, go unto the vessels, and drink of that which the young men have drawn." Charles Ellicott notes the tense — literally, that which they "shall from time to time draw" — a standing permission for the whole harvest; and he permits himself the evocative conjecture that the water came "possibly from that self-same well at Bethlehem from which David desired to drink" (2 Sam. 23:15). Matthew Henry makes the same connection: Boaz bids her drink of the water drawn for his own servants, "drawn from the famous well of Beth-lehem which was by the gate, the water of which David longed for." The image quietly links the Moabite gleaner to her great-grandson before he is born.

Several witnesses pause over the social inversion. Drawing water was the stranger's and the servant's task — Ruth might have expected to draw for others; instead the young men of a wealthy Israelite draw, and the daughter of Moab drinks. John Gill notes the practical directive that frames it all — her eyes are to be upon the field being reaped, following after the reapers and gathering what they drop — so that the kindnesses are fitted precisely to her work: safety while she labours, water because she labours. The pastoral point on which the witnesses converge is that true generosity studies its object. Boaz does not fling Ruth a gift and dismiss her; he thinks through her day — where she will walk, what she will fear, when she will thirst — and orders his household around her need. Henry's Boaz is the pattern of masters who see that those under them do no hurt as well as suffer none; and behind him the witnesses discern a greater Kinsman, in whose field the weakest gleaner is charged upon, guarded from the destroyer, and given freely of water she did not draw.
10:
Ruth's response is a prostration and a question, and the witnesses find both full of meaning. John Gill reads the falling on her face as "great humility, and under a deep sense of the favour done her" — the utmost respect she could show in a civil manner. Adam Clarke explains the gesture from Eastern custom: she "prostrated herself, as was the custom in the East when inferiors approached those of superior rank." Keil and Delitzsch compare Abigail before David in 1 Sam. 25:23, and fix the key verb of her question: to "take knowledge" of someone is "to look at with sympathy or care, to receive a person kindly." John Wesley observes that this was "the humblest posture of reverence, either civil when performed to men, or religious, when to God," and glosses her words: why shouldest thou "shew any respect and kindness to me?"

The sting of the question lies in its last word: "seeing I am a stranger." Charles Ellicott notes that the term means a foreigner outright, and adds the philologist's aside that the Moabite tongue "really differed but little from Hebrew," as the Mesha inscription discovered in 1868 shows — Ruth could be understood in Bethlehem, yet heard as a Moabitess in every syllable. Clarke preserves the Targum's remarkable expansion of the scene, in which Ruth presses the point to its legal edge: how have I found grace, "who am a stranger and one of the daughters of Moab, of whom it is said, The unclean shall not enter into the congregation of the Lord?" (Deut. 23:3) — and Boaz answers that the decree fell on the men of Moab, not the women, and that it had been told him by prophecy "that kings and prophets shall proceed from thee because of the good which thou hast done." Whatever the worth of the paraphrase, the witnesses value what it sees: the verse turns on the apparent impossibility of a Moabite finding grace in Israel, and on the God who overrules the impossibility.

Matthew Henry gathers the practical fruit. "Good breeding is a great ornament to religion; and we must render honour to whom honour is due" — Ruth's grace of manner adorns her grace of heart. More deeply, she gratefully acknowledged the kindness and humbly owned herself unworthy of it; though Boaz had done little more than the divine law obliged, "yet she magnifies and admires it." Herein the witnesses hear the authentic accent of every soul surprised by mercy. Ruth's question — why have I found grace, who am a stranger? — is, they observe, the question grace always provokes and never fully answers; and the wonder is not lessened but deepened when the stranger learns, as this one will, that the kindness was purposed before ever she asked.
11:
Boaz's reply reveals that Ruth's sacrifice was already famous. John Gill passes on Alshech's observation that Boaz "lift up his voice that all that stood by might hear" — the commendation was deliberately public, restoring honour to a woman who had just called herself a stranger. What had been "fully shewed" him was twofold: her devotion to her mother-in-law since her husband's death, and her emigration — "how thou hast left thy father and thy mother, and the land of thy nativity, and art come unto a people which thou knewest not heretofore." Charles Ellicott relishes the Hebrew idiom behind that last word: literally, a people thou knewest not "yesterday and the day before."

It is Keil and Delitzsch who name the resemblance every reader half feels: in leaving father, mother, and kindred for an unknown people, Ruth "has therefore done what God commanded Abraham to do" in Gen. 12:1. The Moabite widow stands in the line of the patriarch himself — her journey from Moab to Bethlehem a small Gentile exodus of faith, undertaken with no promise in hand except the God of a bereaved old woman. Boaz's next words in Ruth 2:12, echoing the language of Abraham's exceeding great reward in Gen. 15:1, confirm that he is thinking in exactly these terms; and the witnesses find in his whole speech "the genuine piety of a true Israelite," who recognizes the faith of Abraham wherever it appears, even in a daughter of Moab.

Matthew Henry draws out both duties enclosed in the verse. Toward those who do well: "Those that do well ought to have the praise of it" — Boaz does not merely feel admiration, he speaks it, and to her face. Toward converts above all: citing the Chaldee paraphrase, "Thou hast come to be proselyted, and to dwell among a people whom thou knewest not," Henry concludes that "those that leave all, to embrace the true religion, are worthy of double honour." The witnesses note, too, what the scene says about testimony. Ruth had never advertised herself; her deeds were reported by others — the townswomen, the servants, the common talk of Bethlehem — until they reached the one man who could reward them. There is encouragement here, they urge, for every obscure fidelity: kindness done to an aged widow in a private house had been observed by God and, through unnoticed channels, "fully shewed" to the redeemer. Nothing done for love's sake is lost. The record travels ahead of the doer, and the God who saw Ruth's hidden years in Moab was preparing, all the while, the field, the kinsman, and the public word of honour that here begins to repay them.
12:
Boaz's blessing is the theological summit of the chapter, and the witnesses climb it from several sides. Keil and Delitzsch trace its scriptural texture: "the Lord recompense thy work" recalls the reward promised to Abraham in Gen. 15:1, and the figure of wings is drawn from Deut. 32:11, where the Lord bears Israel as an eagle her young — compare Ps. 91:4 and Ps. 57:1. Adam Clarke unfolds the image in homelier fashion: it is "taken from the young of fowls, who, seeing a bird of prey, run to their mother to be covered by her wings from danger, and also to take shelter from storms." John Wesley allows both registers: the wings signify "protection and care," an allusion "either to hens, which protect and cherish their young ones under their wings; or to the wings of the Cherubim, between which God dwelt" — the farmyard and the holy of holies offering the same shelter.

On the theology of recompense the witnesses are careful and unanimous. John Gill states the Reformed balance exactly: God is not unrighteous to forget the work and labour of love (Heb. 6:10) shown by children to parents, "and though such works are not in themselves meritorious of any blessing from God here or hereafter, yet he is pleased of his own grace to recompence them." Clarke hears the same modesty in Boaz himself: "The kindness I show thee is little in comparison of thy desert; God alone can give thee a full reward" — the rich man deflecting gratitude upward. Charles Ellicott compresses the promise: "Faith in Divine help and grace will win an undoubted recompense." And Matthew Henry supplies the evangelical assurance: "Those that by faith come under the wings of the divine grace, and have a full complacency and confidence in that grace, may be sure of a full recompence of reward for their so doing" — noting that from this very expression the Jews came to describe a proselyte as one "gathered under the wings of the divine majesty."

All the witnesses agree on what the blessing presupposes: Ruth's conversion is already complete. Clarke says plainly, "It is evident from this that Ruth had already attached herself to the Jewish religion"; her coming to trust under the Lord's wings is Boaz's description of the vow of Ruth 1:16, and Keil and Delitzsch hear in the whole speech the piety of a true Israelite. What none of the actors yet knows — and what the witnesses delight to point out the story will reveal — is that Boaz's prayer will be answered chiefly through Boaz. When Ruth asks him in Ruth 3:9 to spread his wing over his handmaid, she will be claiming from the man the very shelter he here asks God to give her. He who blesses the refugee becomes the wing under which she rests; so, the witnesses conclude, God ordinarily repays trust in his protection — through persons whose kindness he has himself prepared.
13:
Ruth's answer keeps the exchange in perfect balance — grateful, humble, and quietly hopeful — but the witnesses divide over the shading of her first clause. John Gill takes it as acknowledgment rather than petition: the words "are to be considered, not as a wish for it, but as acknowledging it, and expressing her faith and confidence, that she should for time to come find favour in his sight" — he would render it, "I shall find favour." Keil and Delitzsch read the same verb the other way: it "is to be taken in an optative sense, as expressive of the wish that Boaz might continue towards her the kindness he had already expressed" — "May I find favour in thine eyes." The difference is small but real: on one reading Ruth speaks the assurance of faith, on the other the modesty of hope. John Wesley sides in effect with the latter: "I humbly implore the continuance of thy good opinion of me, though I do not deserve it."

On the ground of her plea all agree. She gives two reasons: "thou hast comforted me," and "thou hast spoken friendly unto thine handmaid." Charles Ellicott uncovers the Hebrew warmth beneath the English: "friendly" is literally "unto the heart," the same phrase rendered "comfortably" in Isa. 40:2, where God speaks to the heart of Jerusalem — Boaz has done for Ruth what the prophet's God does for his exiled people. Then comes her self-effacing close, "though I be not like unto one of thine handmaidens," and Keil and Delitzsch quote Carpzov's fine paraphrase: "But what am I saying when I call myself thy maiden? since I am not worthy to be compared to the least of thy maidens." Adam Clarke puts it simply: "I am as unworthy of thy regards as any of thine own maidservants, and yet thou showest me distinguished kindness." Wesley enumerates what lay behind the sentence: she was "mean, necessitous, and obscure, a stranger, and one born of heathen parents, and not of the holy and honourable people of Israel."

The witnesses linger on the phrase "spoken to the heart" as the verse's treasure. Matthew Henry, observing that Ruth owns Boaz's words to have been "a cordial to her," presses the application upon all who have power or standing: "Those that are great, and in high places, know not how much good they may do to their inferiors with a kind look or by speaking friendly to them; and so small an expense, one would think, they should not grudge." Words cost Boaz nothing and were to Ruth food and medicine. Whether her reply be read as confidence or as entreaty, the witnesses find in it the temper that grace forms: a soul that receives kindness without presuming on it, values words above gifts, and answers honour with a lowlier estimate of itself.
14:
The kindness now moves from words to bread, and the witnesses savour every item of the meal. On the "vinegar," Keil and Delitzsch explain the chomets as "a sour beverage composed of vinegar (wine vinegar or sour wine) mixed with oil; a very refreshing drink, which is still a favourite beverage in the East." Adam Clarke concurs from the travellers: "some refreshing kind of acid sauce used by the reapers to dip their bread in, which both cooled and refreshed them," the custom of dipping "the bread and hand together" surviving among the Arabs to his own day. Charles Ellicott adds two biblical sidelights: such sour wine was forbidden to Nazarites (Num. 6:3), and it was similar to the vinegar, mingled with water, that was offered to our Saviour (Matt. 27:48) — the harvest cordial of Bethlehem reappearing at the cross of Bethlehem's greatest Son. The "parched corn," Keil and Delitzsch note, was roasted grains of wheat still eaten by reapers on the harvest fields; Clarke calls it "a frequent repast among the ancients in almost all countries."

The manners of the meal speak as loudly as the menu. John Wesley notices where Ruth placed herself: "She sat — not with or among them, but at some little distance from them, as one inferior to them"; and of Boaz's response he says, "It is no disparagement to the finest hand, to be reached forth to the needy." For Keil and Delitzsch, Ruth's "unassuming humility... made Boaz all the more favourably disposed towards her": he calls her to the common table and with his own hand reaches her the parched corn. Matthew Henry widens the scene into a theology of the table. Boaz offers not bread only but sauce, "for God allows us not only nourishing but relishing food, not for necessity only, but for delight"; the master's hand stretched out to a gleaner recalls the virtuous woman of Prov. 31:20 who reacheth forth her hands to the needy; and Boaz's abundance — providing for his reapers so much beyond their need that a stranger could be entertained — illustrates "there is that scattereth and yet increaseth" (Prov. 11:24).

Finally the witnesses observe how Ruth ate: "she did eat, and was sufficed, and left." Henry reads temperance and forethought together: "She did not, under pretence either of her want or of her labour, eat more than was convenient for her, nor so much as to unfit her for work in the afternoon. Temperance is a friend to industry." Ellicott notes that what she "left" — had to spare — was deliberately "put by for her mother-in-law," as Ruth 2:18 discloses. Even at another's table Ruth remains what she has been all chapter: modest in taking, diligent in working, and mindful of Naomi at home; while Boaz's meal, the witnesses agree, sketches in miniature the Lord who bids the poor sit down, serves them with his own hand, and gives them more than enough.
15:
Two movements share the verse, and the witnesses attend to both. First, Ruth's: "when she was risen up to glean." John Gill notes that as soon as she had eaten and refreshed herself, "she rose up from her seat to go into the field and glean again; which shows her industry" — no lingering at the master's table, no presuming that favour had ended her workday. The kindness she had received sent her back to her task, not away from it.

Second, Boaz's: the quiet command to his young men, "Let her glean even among the sheaves, and reproach her not." Here the witnesses measure how far beyond the law his kindness has now gone. Adam Clarke explains the concession: "This was a privilege; for no person should glean till the sheaves were all bound, and the shocks set up" — the gleaner's right began only where the harvesters had wholly finished, but Ruth may now gather in the very midst of the standing sheaves, where the pickings were richest. Keil and Delitzsch underline that gleaning between the sheaves "was not generally allowed," and render the second clause, "ye shall not shame her" — no scolding, no insinuation, no wounding word for the foreigner enjoying an irregular liberty. Matthew Henry paraphrases the protection precisely: the servants were "not to reproach her, that is, not to call her thief, or to suspect her of taking more than was allowed her." Boaz guards her honour as carefully as her gleaning; the privilege would have been poisoned if every handful had cost her a sneer.

Keil and Delitzsch then draw the conclusion toward which the whole scene has been moving: "These directions of Boaz went far beyond the bounds of generosity and compassion for the poor; and show that he felt a peculiar interest in Ruth, with whose circumstances he was well acquainted, and who had won his heart by her humility, her faithful attachment to her mother-in-law, and her love to the God of Israel" — a fact, they add, important for the further course of the history. The commentator says soberly what the narrative implies: this is more than charity now, and chapters three and four are already in germ. The witnesses find in the verse a picture of grace outrunning entitlement. The law gave Ruth the corners and the leavings; love admits her among the sheaves. And because true generosity attends to the spirit as well as the letter, the same word that enlarges her portion silences every voice that might have made her ashamed to take it. The gleaner works on, unaware how much of her plenty is by design — which is, the witnesses observe, very often the condition of those most blessed.
16:
The command rises to its most delicate pitch: "let fall also some of the handfuls of purpose for her." John Gill explains the mechanics of the contrivance: the reapers, "when they had reaped an handful, instead of laying it in its proper order, to be taken up by those that gathered after them, or by themselves, in order to be bound up in sheaves," were to "scatter it about, or let it fall where they reaped it" — good grain deliberately dropped in the gleaner's path. Keil and Delitzsch render the further clause: "ye shall also draw out of the bundles for her, and let them lie... that she may glean them, and shall not scold her" — even ears already bound were to be pulled out and left, and no servant might rebuke her for picking up what had been secretly disbursed for her sake.

The witnesses are struck above all by the form the generosity takes. Boaz does not hand Ruth a sack of barley, though nothing would have been easier for a mighty man of wealth. He arranges instead that her own gleaning should prosper — that she should carry home at evening what looked, to her eye, like the fruit of her own hands. Matthew Henry states the principle: "The greatest kindness we can do our poor relations is to assist and encourage their industry"; Boaz "encouraged her to go on in her gleaning, and did not offer to take her off from that." Charity of this kind feeds the body without starving the spirit; it preserves in the receiver the dignity of labour while quietly guaranteeing its success. Henry adds a word for the household: though masters may restrain their servants' wastefulness, "they should not tie them up from being charitable, but give them allowance for that, with prudent directions" — Boaz's men are authorized, indeed commanded, to be wasteful on purpose.

Later devotion has loved this verse for its picture of providence, and the witnesses' exposition shows why. What Ruth found lying in the stubble was, so far as she could tell, fortune — a lucky field, a careless reaper; in fact it was "of purpose," love working anonymously. The witnesses invite the believer to read much personal history in the same light: mercies that seemed chance windfalls were handfuls let fall by a hand that gave its orders in the morning. And the manner is as instructive as the fact. The Lord of the harvest, like Boaz, commonly blesses his poor not by exempting them from the field but by strewing the field before them — meeting diligence with an abundance it never earned, and forbidding any voice, even the accusing conscience's, to reproach the gleaner for gathering more grace than she can account for.
17:
The verse closes the working day and counts the result, and the witnesses find both instructive. John Gill notes the span: gleaning "until even" was "an instance of her great diligence and industry, attending to this mean employment constantly from morning to night." Matthew Henry develops each clause. She gleaned until evening: we must not be weary of well-doing, because in due season we shall reap (Gal. 6:9); and — a fine observation — "she scarcely used, much less did she abuse, the kindness of Boaz; for, though he ordered his servants to leave handfuls for her, she continued to glean the scattered ears." Favour did not relax her. She beat out what she had gleaned: threshing it that same evening, "that she might the more easily carry it home, and might have it ready for use" — unlike the slothful man who "roasteth not that which he took in hunting" (Prov. 12:27). Charles Ellicott agrees on the practical motive: she threshed it herself "so as to save the labour of carrying away the straw."

Then the measure. Adam Clarke computes the ephah at "not less than seven gallons and a half" and pronounces it "a good day's work"; Ellicott makes it "rather more than four pecks"; Keil and Delitzsch reckon some twenty to twenty-five pounds of barley. However converted, the figure is remarkable for a gleaner — the narrative's own silent testimony both to Ruth's industry and to how many handfuls had fallen of purpose. Henry supplies the homely arithmetic: "Ruth had gathered it ear by ear, but, when she had put it all together, it was an ephah of barley... Many a little makes a great deal. It is an encouragement to industry that in all labour, even that of gleaning, there is profit."

The witnesses follow her home with equal care. She carried the load herself into the city, though Boaz's servants would surely have helped had she asked: "We should study," Henry writes, "to be as little as possible troublesome to those that are kind to us." And she secured what she had won — let us likewise "take care that we lose not those things which we have wrought" (2 John 8). From the ephah the witnesses draw their evening application, which Henry frames as a question for every nightfall: "Where have I gleaned to-day? What improvements have I made in knowledge and grace? What have I done or obtained that will turn to a good account?" The day that began with a request to glean after him in whose sight she should find grace ends with a burden of barley on a widow's shoulder — proof, the witnesses conclude, that diligence and providence are not rivals but partners, the one gathering ear by ear what the other has strewn.
18:
The scene shifts from field to home, and the witnesses follow the barley through the door. John Gill pictures the load: "she took it up" — the whole ephah, on her arms or shoulders — and carried it into the city. Naomi "saw what she had gleaned," and the sight itself spoke: a quantity no ordinary gleaning could explain. Then comes the detail the witnesses love best: "she brought forth, and gave to her that she had reserved after she was sufficed." Keil and Delitzsch supply the picture: she "drew out (from her pocket, as the Chaldee has correctly supplied) what she had left from her sufficiency" — the parched corn Boaz had reached her at the midday meal (Ruth 2:14). Adam Clarke reconstructs the same chain: having "received a distinct portion at dinner-time, of which she had more than she could eat... she brought the rest home to her mother-in-law." John Wesley notes simply that it was reserved "at dinner, after she had eaten and was sufficed, or satisfied."

What moves the witnesses is what the reserving reveals. At the one moment of comfort in her hard day — seated at a rich man's table, served by his own hand — Ruth was thinking of the hungry woman at home. Matthew Henry marks it: "She gave to her what she had reserved... If she had any thing better than another, her mother should have part with her. Thus, having shown industry abroad, she showed piety at home." And he gives the word its scriptural weight: children's maintaining their parents is what the apostle expressly calls piety (1 Tim. 5:4), "and it is part of the honour due to them by the fifth commandment." The ephah proved her diligence; the parched corn proved her love. One was the day's wages, so to speak; the other was pure remembrance, carried through the afternoon's labour for the sake of another's supper.

The witnesses observe how the verse completes the chapter's portrait of Ruth. Every grace has now had its evidence: her humility asked leave to glean; her industry filled the ephah; her temperance was sufficed and stopped; her dutiful affection saved the best morsel for Naomi. Nothing in the day was for herself alone. And they note the quiet reciprocity of providence: the woman who left everything to comfort her mother-in-law's old age is fed at the table of the man who will restore that whole family's inheritance. The parched corn drawn from a gleaner's pocket is, in their reading, the first fruit of the redemption to come — small, humble, and warm from the redeemer's own hand.
19:
Naomi's greeting is a volley of questions and a blessing fired before she knows its target: "Where hast thou gleaned to-day? and where wroughtest thou? blessed be he that did take knowledge of thee." The witnesses reconstruct her evening. Charles Ellicott notes that she "easily perceives that the quantity of corn brought home is unusually large, and that therefore some special kindness must have been shown" — the ephah, and then the parched corn drawn from Ruth's pocket, announced a benefactor before one was named; and so "her own... as well as her daughter's thanks are due" to him. Keil and Delitzsch read the same inference: from the barley collected and the food brought home she conjectured that someone had taken notice of Ruth, and she "praised the benefactor" accordingly. John Gill adds that Naomi supposed Ruth "had been directed by the providence of God to a spot of ground where there was good gleaning," or had met some hand that dropped ears plentifully in her favour — her first instinct is to see God's leading behind the day's success.

Matthew Henry turns the questions into a pattern for households: "Parents should take care to enquire into the ways of their children, how, and where, and in what company they spend their time... If we are not our brethren's, yet surely we are our children's keepers." Yet the enquiry must be of the right temper — "not to frighten nor discourage them, not so as to make them hate home or tempt them to tell a lie, but to commend them if they have done well, and with mildness to reprove and caution them if they have done otherwise." Naomi's question is interest, not interrogation; and Ruth's ready, particular answer — she "shewed her mother in law with whom she had wrought" — models the openness of a child with nothing to hide. Henry also admires the blessing pronounced on a man still unknown: "Blessed be he, whoever he was, that did take knowledge of thee — shooting the arrow of prayer at a venture," gratitude too eager to wait for a name.

Both Henry and John Wesley then turn Naomi's question inward, and their agreement has made it proverbial. "It is a good question to ask ourselves in the evening," Wesley writes: "'Where have I gleaned to-day?' What improvements have I made in grace or knowledge? What have I learned or done, which will turn to account?" The day of a Christian, like the day of a gleaner, is to be audited at nightfall — not in anxiety, but in honest reckoning of what has been gathered. And the verse ends poised on a name: "The man's name with whom I wrought to day is Boaz." The word means nothing yet to Ruth and everything to Naomi; the witnesses leave the reader, with her, at the instant before recognition — when the poor discover that the kindness of a stranger has been, all along, the kindness of a kinsman.
20:
Naomi's outburst of blessing carries the chapter's theology, and the witnesses probe each clause. The first question is grammatical but weighty: who is it "who hath not left off his kindness to the living and to the dead"? Charles Ellicott states the ambiguity frankly: "It is not clear whether the grammatical antecedent is God or Boaz. Either way a good sense is obtained." Matthew Henry and John Wesley read it of Boaz: Naomi "now remembered the former kindnesses Boaz had shown to her husband and sons, and joins those to this" — kindness, as Wesley puts it, "which he formerly shewed to those who are now dead... and now continues to us." Adam Clarke identifies the parties either way: "Naomi and Ruth were the living; and they were also the representatives of Elimelech and Mahlon, who were dead." Yet the ambiguity itself preaches: Boaz's unbroken kindness is the visible edge of the Lord's, and Naomi — who had lately renamed herself Mara under God's hand (Ruth 1:20) — here begins to bless again.

The second clause opens the book's great door: "The man is near of kin unto us, one of our next kinsmen." Clarke expounds the Hebrew: the word is literally "of our redeemers," one who bears the goel's threefold office — "to buy back the forfeited inheritance" or the liberty of the enslaved kinsman, to avenge a kinsman's blood, and "to take the widow of a deceased brother or relative, if he died childless," on pain of public indignity if he refused (Ruth 4:4–10). Ellicott renders it "one of those who must redeem," and remarks how strange it is that Naomi, dwelling near so kindly a kinsman, had never made herself known to him — "a proof of the independence of her character," matching John Gill's earlier observation that modesty had kept her from applying to him. Henry watches the recognition kindle hope, thought by thought: Ruth names her benefactor; Naomi recollects the name; the name recalls the relation; the relation implies the right of redemption — "Thus God brings things to our mind, sometimes on a sudden, that prove to have a wonderful tendency to our good."

From the goel the witnesses' eyes travel, irresistibly, to Christ. Henry says it without apology: "Our blessed Saviour is our Goel; it is he that has a right to redeem." The kinsman who buys back the mortgaged inheritance, frees the enslaved, and takes the widowed and destitute into covenant becomes, in their reading, the Old Testament's living diagram of redemption — nearness of kin answering to the incarnation, the right to redeem to Christ's assumption of our debt. The pastoral turn belongs to Naomi herself: the woman who came home empty discovers that she had a redeemer all along, wealthy, near, and already showing kindness before he was asked. So, the witnesses conclude, the mourner often learns that grace never did leave off — it was at work in the field all day while faith sat grieving at home.
21:
Ruth adds a postscript that shows how carefully she had listened: "He said unto me also, Thou shalt keep fast by my young men, until they have ended all my harvest." John Gill catches its force: besides the favours already shown, Boaz "has given me reason to expect more" — the invitation was not for a day but for the season. Charles Ellicott hears the emphasis in the Hebrew: "my young men... my harvest" — "as long as my reaping lasts, cleave steadily to us"; the stranger is attached, by the master's own word, to the master's own people and the master's own work. John Wesley notes the practical extent: "both barley-harvest, and wheat-harvest" — weeks of secure provision promised in a sentence.

A small verbal question detains the philologists. Ruth says "young men," where Boaz in Ruth 2:8 had said "maidens," and Naomi in the next verse will say "maidens" again. Adam Clarke resolves it by translation: the word "should be translated servants, both the male and female being included in it." Keil and Delitzsch reach the same conclusion grammatically: the masculine is "quite in place as the more comprehensive gender, as a designation of the reapers generally, both male and female," and they draw out the possessive construction — "the people who belong to my house, as distinguished from the people of other masters." Ruth reports the household, not the sex; nothing careless lurks in her phrasing.

Wesley's finer observation concerns what Ruth did not report. "She tells what kindness Boaz had shewed her; but not, how he had commended her. Humility teaches not only not to praise ourselves, but not to be forward in repeating the praise which others have given us." Boaz had publicly celebrated her devotion and prayed heaven's full reward upon her in Ruth 2:11; of all that, Naomi hears nothing. Ruth repeats the practical promise that concerns their bread, and buries the compliments that concerned her honour. The witnesses hold the two halves of the verse together as a single portrait: exact memory for kindness, complete silence about praise. And they observe how the master's word functions for the two widows as promises function for the believer — a security to be leaned on through a whole season of labour. Ruth will still glean ear by ear; the harvest must still be worked to its end; but she works henceforth inside a standing invitation, keeping fast by the people of the man who has pledged her welcome until "all my harvest" is done. Grace, as the witnesses read it, gives not only the day's portion but a place among its own people until the work is finished.
22:
Naomi answers with an older woman's practical wisdom: "It is good, my daughter, that thou go out with his maidens, that they meet thee not in any other field." Keil and Delitzsch uncover the sharpness beneath the mild translation: the verb means "to fall upon a person, to smite and ill-treat him" — Naomi "declared herself fully satisfied with this, because Ruth would be thereby secured from insults, which she might receive when gleaning in strange fields." The mother-in-law knows the world that Boaz's charge in Ruth 2:9 had guarded against. John Wesley adds the second prudence: gleaning elsewhere would both "expose thyself to many inconveniences, which thou mayst expect from strangers; and incur his displeasure, as if thou didst despise his kindness." Charles Ellicott agrees that wandering to other fields "would not only be throwing away genuine kindness, but would be contemptuously proclaiming the fact." Matthew Henry compresses it: to be met elsewhere "will be construed a contempt of his courtesy."

A minor debate attaches to Naomi's wording. Ruth had reported Boaz's invitation with "young men"; Naomi answers with "maidens" — and some interpreters, Henry reports, have found here a tacit rebuke, as though Naomi corrected an impropriety, the maidens being "fitter company for thee than the young men." Henry dismisses the reading — "but they are too critical" — since Ruth had named the young men only as the principal labourers to whom Boaz had given his directions. Ellicott likewise rules that "we need not suppose that any distinction is intended: Ruth names the young men as the chief workers; Naomi, the young women as those with whom Ruth would be specially thrown." The consensus clears Ruth of even a shadow, while conceding that the instinct behind the misreading is sound: modesty keeps company with its own.

It is Henry who lifts the counsel into allegory, and his page here has become the verse's devotional legacy. "Our blessed Saviour is our Goel... If we expect to receive benefit by him, let us closely adhere to him, and his fields, and his family; let us not go to the world and its fields for that which is to be had with him only... Has the Lord dealt bountifully with us? Let us not be found in any other field, nor seek for happiness and satisfaction in the creature." He clinches it with a homely figure: "Tradesmen take it ill if those that are in their books go to another shop. We lose divine favours if we slight them." Naomi's field-wisdom thus becomes the witnesses' rule of perseverance: the soul that has found grace in one field — the Redeemer's — shows both gratitude and sense by being found nowhere else until the harvest ends.
23:
The chapter closes with a sentence of quiet faithfulness, and the witnesses read it as the seal on Ruth's character. John Gill gathers the threads: accepting Boaz's kindness, attending to Naomi's advice, and labouring for them both, she was "a wonderful instance of dutiful affection, humility, and industry." Keil and Delitzsch mark the duration — "the whole of the barley and wheat harvests" — and detect the narrator's art in the closing words, "and dwelt with her mother in law": "In this last remark there is a tacit allusion to the fact that a change took place for Ruth when the harvest was over." The sentence that sounds like an ending is a hinge; chapter three waits behind it. Charles Ellicott admires what the long season proved: "Unspoiled by mixing with her new society, she stops on quietly at the end of her task, and tends her mother-in-law at home with the same fidelity with which she had worked for her abroad."

Matthew Henry fills in the domestic picture. She gleaned to the end of both harvests, gathering food in harvest against the winter like the wise provider of Prov. 6:6–8; she "constantly came to her mother at night in due time, as became a virtuous woman, that was for working days, and not for merry nights"; and when harvest ended she "did not gad abroad, but kept her aged mother company at home." He points the contrast that Israel's memory supplied: "Dinah went out to see the daughters of the land," and her vanity ended in disgrace (Gen. 34); Ruth kept at home, "and we shall find afterwards what preferment her humility and industry ended in. Seest thou a man diligent in his business? Honour is before him" (Prov. 22:29).

Adam Clarke supplies the widest horizon. Ruth's modesty, industry, and attachment to Naomi "all worked together in the course of Providence, to bring about a matrimonial connection, which in its issue was intimately connected with the salvation of a lost world; for, from this very line, Jesus Christ, according to the flesh, sprang" (Matt. 1:5) — and he ventures that Ruth "showed herself as worthy to be one of His progenitors as the Virgin Mary was to be His mother." His closing counsel binds providence to duty: had Ruth not walked as she walked, "how dreary and uncomfortable must her life have been!" — but she "followed God fully," and found the path of duty to be the way of safety. "Acknowledge him in all thy ways, and he will direct thy steps" (Prov. 3:6); "Trust in the Lord with thy whole heart" (Prov. 3:5). So the witnesses leave the gleaner at Naomi's fireside: an ordinary summer of unnoticed diligence, out of which God was quietly building the house of David and the genealogy of his Son.
